coredata.dc:title: Crosslinked carboxymethyl cellulose colloidal solution for cotton thread coating in wound dressing: A rheological study
coredata.dc:creator: Khairunnisa-Atiqah, Mohamad Khalid
Salleh, Kushairi Mohd
Hair, Ainul Hafiza Abd
Mazlan, Nyak Syazwani Nyak
Mostapha, Marhaini
Zakaria, Sarani
coredata.dc:description: Cotton thread therapeutic properties as a wound dressing can be enhanced by utilising carboxymethyl cellulose-nanoparticles (CMC/NPs) colloidal solution as a coating solution. Nanoparticles such as graphene oxide (GO), graphene quantum dots (GQD), and silver nanoparticles (AgNP) stability in CMC was investigated through the rheological analysis and UV–Vis spectroscopy of the colloidal solutions. Citric acid (CA) acted as a crosslinker and was utilised to crosslink the colloidal solution with cotton thread. These CMC/NPs coated threads were subjected to mechanical properties and antibacterial activity analysis. Results obtained indicate less nanoparticle agglomeration and were stable in the CMC-based nanofluid. CMC/NPs rheological study suggested that colloidal solutions exhibited shear thinning behaviour and behaved as non-Newtonian fluids with n < 1. Crosslinked CMC/NPs appeared in a gel-like state as the viscoelasticity of the solution increased. Among the colloidal solutions, CMC/AgNP showed the highest enhancement with a significant difference at p < 0.05 in terms of mechanical and antibacterial properties. Consequently, the rheological properties and stability of CMC/NPs might influence the coating solution's appearance and refine the cotton thread's microstructure for a functional wound dressing to be further utilised as a coating solution for antibacterial cotton thread wound dressing material.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cotton thread therapeutic properties as a wound dressing can be enhanced by utilising carboxymethyl cellulose-nanoparticles (CMC/NPs) colloidal solution as a coating solution. Nanoparticles such as graphene oxide (GO), graphene quantum dots (GQD), and silver nanoparticles (AgNP) stability in CMC was investigated through the rheological analysis and UV–Vis spectroscopy of the colloidal solutions. Citric acid (CA) acted as a crosslinker and was utilised to crosslink the colloidal solution with cotton thread. These CMC/NPs coated threads were subjected to mechanical properties and antibacterial activity analysis. Results obtained indicate less nanoparticle agglomeration and were stable in the CMC-based nanofluid. CMC/NPs rheological study suggested that colloidal solutions exhibited shear thinning behaviour and behaved as non-Newtonian fluids with n < 1. Crosslinked CMC/NPs appeared in a gel-like state as the viscoelasticity of the solution increased. Among the colloidal solutions, CMC/AgNP showed the highest enhancement with a significant difference at p < 0.05 in terms of mechanical and antibacterial properties. Consequently, the rheological properties and stability of CMC/NPs might influence the coating solution's appearance and refine the cotton thread's microstructure for a functional wound dressing to be further utilised as a coating solution for antibacterial cotton thread wound dressing material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cotton thread has been famously used as a wound dressing material such as gauze and bandages due to its hygroscopic, biocompatible, and soft properties. Nonetheless, cotton thread is unable to provide overall protection due to lack of antibacterial properties which can lead to infection in the wound area. Additionally, cotton threads tend to adhere to the wound, causing secondary trauma during removal and have limited absorbency [1]. An efficient wound dressing crucial properties should include the ability to retain exudate and prevent further damage from infections that hinder wound healing [2]. Modern-day dressing focuses on providing the optimal condition to accelerate wound healing [3] such as maintaining moisture, providing good aeration for gas exchange, preventing bacterial colonisation, and providing mechanical support for protection [2].

A well-known cellulose derivative which has been famously used in biomedical applications is carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC). CMC is a cellulose ether with excellent water-solubility and a linear long-chain cellulose backbone [4] that contains an additional carboxymethyl group (–CH2-COOH) [5] bounded to the hydroxyl group (–OH) of the glucopyranose chain of cellulose [6]. CMC is used for its ability to act as a binder due to the molecule's electrostatic repulsion in an aqueous solution created by the repellence of the adjacent CMC chains. This repellence allows high-viscosity colloidal solution formation [7]. For wound dressing application, CMC salt derivatives, sodium CMC (NaCMC) is widely used due to its non-toxic to humans and all animal species [6,8]. Furthermore, dressing coated with CMC appears to be more flexible, has better exudate absorbing ability, and can promote angiogenesis in wound healing [9]. Apart from that, CMC-based wound dressing has high film-forming capability, excellent compatibility, and accelerates the wound healing process [10]. Previous studies indicated that due to CMC's unique chemical structure, highly advantageous properties, and tuneable rheological properties [11], CMC has been highlighted as a desirable biomedical coating material [12] because active CMC-based coating contains texture modifier to prevent physical damages and inhibits microbial colonisation [13]. CMC as a rheological modifier also has been of growing interest due to its unique ability to enhance viscosity and elasticity in biomedical coatings while also exhibiting excellent stability and compatibility with other materials [7]. This finding is supported by a study conducted by Rakhmatullayeva et al. which indicated that the nanofilm coated onto the surgical suture showed an increase in elasticity and stability with high antibacterial activity [14]. The stability of CMC coating solution also can be incorporated with antibiotics, bioactive compounds and nanoparticles to improve coated material's antibacterial activity [14,15].

Graphene oxide (GO) with sp2 hybridization carbon atoms [16] is the product of 2D graphene sheet oxidation. Meanwhile, zero-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is derived from the graphene family that consists of a single to a few layers of graphene sheets with a lateral dimension of <10 nm [17]. GO and GQDs share the same sp2 network carbon atoms pattern, but the carbon atoms in GQDs are arranged in a hexagonal lattice structure [18]. GO and GQDs have a large specific surface area attributed to their size. Along with a plethora of oxygenated functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy at both GO and GQDs basal plane [19] allows them to have stable dispersion in water. Moreover, GQDs can improve their solubility and stability in solution due to GQDs can be functionalized with various chemical groups, resulting in the shifting of GQD's photoluminescence properties [20]. GO and GQDs are also merited for their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high solubility [21] allowing them to be utilised in antibacterial applications. The application of GO and GQDs aids in wound bed infection prevention where GO acts as a nanoblade, and GQD adheres to the bacteria cell surface and induces the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [22,23]. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) need no introduction when it comes to their application in biomedical. Its physicochemical properties such as its nanosize which provide a large surface area-to-volume ratio, allow a lot of contact sites for various reactions [24]. AgNPs have two antibacterial modes of action. The first mode involved the inhibition action, where the silver ion (Ag+) inhibits the respiratory enzyme, leading to the formation of ROS. The second mode is the bactericidal activity occurred when AgNPs bind to the sulphur and phosphorus groups of DNA structure, causing DNA damage leading to apoptosis, and preventing DNA replication, thus killing the bacteria [25].

The rheological study of CMC colloidal solutions for biocoating applications offer several advantages including the correlation how rheological properties can complement other characteristics such as biocompatibility, absorption capability, drug release profiles, and mechanical strength. For example, elastic modulus can provide insights into its ability to conform to wound contours and resist deformation. Ergo, this study aims to evaluate GO, GQDs and AgNP stability in CMC solution along with CMC/NP colloidal solution's rheological properties. It is hypothesised that CMC can stabilise these nanoparticles and reduces nanoparticle agglomeration, thus forming stable colloidal solutions. The rheological properties of CMC/NPs colloidal solution can provide a better understanding to utilise CMC/NPs colloidal solution as a coating agent for cotton thread. Therefore, cotton thread coated with CMC/NPs may have mechanical and antibacterial properties enhancement.

Solid powder sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (Na-CMC) (average Mw: ~700,000) with medium viscosity, and a degree of substitution of 0.65–0.90, citric acid (anhydrous, Mw: 192.12), and silver nanoparticles were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). The graphene oxide was purchased from GO Advance Solutions Sdn. Bhd. (1206928-P) (Selangor, Malaysia) and graphene quantum dots (SKU: GNQD0101) was purchased from ACS Material LLC (Pasadena, California, USA). 100 % mercerized cotton thread was purchased from Coats Cotton (Hungary, Europe).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CMC and CMC/NPs colloidal solution preparation
ce:section.ce:para: NaCMC powder was dissolved gradually in distilled water (dH2O) at room temperature (RT) and was stirred until a homogenous aqueous CMC solution (2 % w/v) was obtained. The CMC solution concentration was obtained based on the previous reported study by Khairunnisa-atiqah et al. 2022 [28]. GO, GQD and AgNP (1 % w/v) were dispersed in 30 mL CMC solutions in separated containers and were sonicated in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min, followed by stirring for 2 h to obtain CMC+GO, CMC+GQD, and CMC+AgNP homogenous colloidal solution. The concentration of the nanoparticles were determined based on previously reported studies whereby 1 % of GO, GQD, and AgNP is sufficient for application in biomedical coating applications [32–36]. Once homogenous colloidal solutions has been obtained, the colloidal solutions were degassed for 30 min to eliminate bubbles. CA (2M) were added into the colloidal solutions and were heated at 90°C to form crosslinked CMC/GO, CMC/GQD, and CMC/AgNP colloidal solutions. All nanoparticles have a final concentration of 1 mg/mL.

A sedimentation test was performed to study the stability of the colloidal solutions. A sedimentation test was performed in dH2O and CMC solution where dH2O was controlled. A sedimentation test was performed by mixing 1 % (w/v) of nanoparticles (GO, GQD and AgNP) into CMC solution forming respective colloidal solutions forming an uncrosslinked colloidal solution indicated by CMC+GO, CMC+GQD, and CMC+AgNP, respectively. After thoroughly mixing, the colloidal solutions were transferred into a cuvette and the colloidal solution was measured to obtain the initial absorbance using with UV‐Vis spectrometer. After the initial absorbance was measured, the colloidal solution was placed statically to initiate gravitational sedimentation. The sedimentation test was conducted for 48 h, and the colloidal solution absorbance was measured every 2 h. UV–Vis spectroscopy (Jenway Spectrophotometer JEN0001 Ver 1.2, Cole-Parmer) was used to confirm the presence of GO, GQDs, and AgNPs in CMC-colloidal solutions. The absorbance spectrum of the colloidal samples was obtained in the range of 200–800 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological property of CMC/NPs colloidal solution
ce:section.ce:para: The viscosity of the colloidal solution was measured using Discovery HR-2 Hybrid Rheometer (TA Instrument, New Castle, Delaware, USA) and analyzed using TA Instrument Trios (version 5.1.1.46572). The shear rate and temperature for the static rheological measurement were set at 0.1 to 100 s−1 for 25 °C. The effect of temperature was studied at 25°C–90°C. The frequency for the dynamic rheological measurements was set in a range from 1 to 100 rad/s at 25°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial inhibition of CMC/NPs colloidal solutions
ce:section.ce:para:
The antibacterial inhibition was performed using broth method was adapted to quantify the CMC/NP colloidal solutions growth inhibition against S. aureus and E. coli. 2 mL of Luria-Bertani (LB) broth was added into empty test tubes labelled negative control (−'ve), positive control (+'ve), CMC with CA (CMC+CA), CMC/GO, CMC/GQD, and CMC/AgNP. The inhibition activity was carried out as shown in Fig. 1 . CMC/NPs colloidal solution (2 mL) was added into the LB broth and mixed well using a Heidolph vortex mixer (Germany). Once homogenized, 0.2 mL of 104 bacterial culture dilution was added into all tubes except for the control tube. The test tubes were all incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. The bacterial growth in all test tubes was determined by measuring the turbidity of cultures using a UV–VIS spectrophotometer at 600 nm and the optical density (OD) values were recorded. The bacterial inhibition percentage was calculated using Eq. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cotton thread coating with CMC/NPs colloidal solution
ce:section.ce:para: Cotton threads were soaked and washed in 2 % sodium lauryl sulfonate (C12H28NaO3S) solution for 15 min, followed by ultrasonic washing for 30 min. After ultrasonic washing, the threads were rinsed with dH2O followed by 80 % ethanol (EtOH) for 1 h and rinsed again with dH2O for 30 min. This step was repeated 3 times. After washing, the threads were dried using an infrared (IR) dryer at 60 °C for 30 min. Once dried the cotton threads were inserted through a 20-gauge needle attached to a 25 mL syringe containing each CMC/NPs colloidal solution. The cotton threads were soaked for 18 h in each colloidal solution individually. After soaking, the threads were pulled out from the syringe and were immediately immersed into a container containing 2M CA. The threads were immersed for 1 h at 90 °C to promote crosslinking reaction [28]. After immersion, filtered paper was used to remove excess CA, and the thread was dried using IR drying at 70 °C for 45 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR)
ce:section.ce:para: Alpha Platinum-ATR Spectrometer (Bruker, Massachusetts, United States) was used to measure the infrared spectra. The samples were scanned in the mid-IR spectra within the wavenumber 400–4000 cm−1 range and recorded at 32 scans with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

The tensile strength (TS) and modulus of elasticity (MOE) were carried out using Servo Control System Desktop Tensile Strength Tester AI-3000 N (Qingdao, China) using test method ASTM D3822 (Standard Test Method for Tensile Properties of Single Textile Fibers). The tensile speed was set as constant at 10 mm/min with a 10 kg load cell. Before the test, all samples were conditioned overnight at room temperature under relative humidity at 59.4 % in the drying cabinet (Weifo, Taiwan). The length of the samples is 70 mm. Each sample's end was secured with masking tape and inserted between the clamps with a length of 10 mm to avoid slipping. The distance between each clamp's was set to 50 mm. Ten measurement replicates were done for each sample to obtain the average fibre strength and E. modulus. The tensile strength for each sample was calculated by dividing the average tensile load and fibre diameter.

The stability of the colloidal solution can be predicted through visual observation where the nanoparticles sedimentation presents at the bottom part of the cuvette containing colloidal solution due to poor dispersion ability and low stability. Nanoparticle sedimentation was checked by mixing three different nanoparticles with dH2O and CMC at equal time intervals as shown in Fig. 2(a–d).

During the initial stage of the measurement, the colloidal solution appears to be homogenized and the nanoparticles are uniformly distributed. Fig. 2(b) displayed the colloidal solution after 12 h of test. From the observation, it is apparent that GO and AgNP nanoparticles showed complete sedimentation in the dH2O-based solution. This can be caused by the gravitational and van der Waals forces [38,39]. From Fig. 2, it is found that CMC-based nanofluids exhibit higher stability than dH2O-based nanofluids, with CMC+AgNP giving maximum stability than CMC+GO. This can be explained by the less agglomeration of AgNP in CMC-based nanofluid due to the high repulsive force between AgNP and CMC in the colloidal solution that leads to the stabilization of AgNP [40]. Furthermore, CMC-based nanofluids act as stabilizing and reducing agents allowing CMC to reduce AgNP and forming a layer around the nanoparticles to prevent their agglomeration [7]. After 24 h, CMC-based nanofluids appear to be more stable compared to dH2O-based nanofluids (Fig. 2(c)) as CMC acts as a surfactant which is an effective chemical method to enhance nanofluids stability [41]. The nanoparticle sedimentation after 48 h exhibited total sedimentation in dH2O-based nanofluids, in contrast to CMC-based nanofluids (Fig. 2(d)). Due to the GQD size nanosize and fluorescence property, the dispersion cannot be observed physically with naked eyes under normal light conditions.

To validate the observation in Fig. 2, UV–VIS photometric analysis was done to monitor nanofluid stability. The sedimentation can be detected by the decrease of absorbance peak. The photometric analysis was done according to the nanoparticles wavelength which are GO (235 and 300 nm), GQD (275 and 365 nm), and AgNP (465 nm). In contrast to nanofluids in which nanoparticles are the onset of sedimentation in dH2O, the absorption peak of freshly prepared nanofluids is greater, as seen in Fig. 3(a). Whereas Fig. 3(b) shows the stability of the CMC+NPs colloidal solutions. From the figures, it is apparent that CMC can stabilise the GO, GQD and AgNP due to the formation of a stable complex between CMC and the nanoparticles [23,42,43].

The analysis shows that CMC+AgNP showed a significant different at p < 0.05 and have a higher stability in comparison to the CMC+GO and CMC+GQD at their respective wavelength. The AgNPs are stabilized by CMC onto their surface, which can prevent agglomeration [44] hence preventing the AgNP from sediment. Furthermore, the CMC can interact with AgNPs through a chelation process, which can result in the formation of a stable complex (CMC-Ag) [42] which will be discussed further in the next section. The stability of the CMC+AgNP colloidal solution permits the improvement of coated material in terms of mechanical and antibacterial properties [27].

Fig. 4(a) shows that the GO absorption peak is at ~230 and ~300 nm. Peak ~230 nm represents the electronic π → π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds, while peak ~300 nm indicates the n → π* transitions of CO bonds [45]. The absorption spectrum of CMC/GO colloidal solution exhibits a slight shift from ~230 to ~235 nm, indicating the presence of an unsaturated carbon ring and oxygen-containing functional group within the structure [46], suggesting GO partly reduction during CMC/GO colloidal solution preparation and the graphene sheets electronic conjugation recovery simultaneously [47].

From Fig. 4(b), GQD exhibited absorption band at 270–280 nm and 365 nm which is typical for GQD that is associated with π–π* transition of sp 2 carbon domain in sp 3 carbon matrix and n–π* transition of CO [48]. At 365 nm, the UV absorption is related to electron transition from n to π*of CC and visible absorption relates to π electrons partial conjugation in GQD layers [49]. However, at 490 nm shoulder peak appears for CMC/GQD which is absent in CMC + GQD. CMC/GQD colloidal solution showed a peak at 490 nm associated with the presence of n–π* transition in conjugated CN bond that is also associated with pyrrolic-N unique character in GQDs [50].

For AgNPs, as shown in Fig. 4(c), AgNP absorbs radiation in the visible light region of the electromagnetic spectrum between ~420 and 480 nm due to silver-containing sample association with the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) vibration of AgNPs [51,52]. AgNP fabrication into CMC is investigated by surface plasmon resonance (SPR) analysis which is a spectral fingerprint for plasmonic nanoparticles [53]. At λmax = 465 nm a peak was obtained for CMC/AgNP colloidal solution due to the excitation of SPR that explains the combined excitation of conduction electrons in metal [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological properties of CMC/NPs colloidal solution
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of shear rate and temperature on the viscosity of CMC colloidal solutions was investigated using static rheological properties. The dynamic rheological measurement may be used to determine the storage and loss modulus, as well as the complicated viscosity of CMC colloidal solutions.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the static rheological characteristics of CMC/NPs colloidal solution can be studied by examining the effects of shear rate and effect temperature on the CMC/NPs colloidal solution. Fig. 5(a) shows that the viscosity of all samples reduces as the shear rate increases. The decrease in viscosity values indicates that the crosslinked CMC/NPs colloidal solutions exhibit shear thinning or pseudoplastic behaviour. This stipulates that there are many entanglement points between nanoparticles and the CMC matrix as the crosslinking process occurs and as the shear rate increases, the entanglement begins to dissemble, leading to the reduction in CMC/NPs colloidal solutions viscosity.

CMC can exhibits both Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluid behaviour. CMC often behaves like a Newtonian fluid in low concentration (~1 %) and non-Newtonian fluid at a concentration higher than 1 % (2–5 %), which supports the characteristic of 2 % CMC exhibited in this study [7]. The shear thinning behaviour of crosslinked CMC/NPs colloidal solutions indicates a sharp and high flow behaviour. The shear thinning behaviour will also be attributed to other properties such as pseudoplasticity and viscoelasticity behaviour. The entanglement between CMC and nanoparticles can also be attributed to CMC molecular structure which is responsible for the viscoelasticity properties due to CMC having a long chain structure and high entanglement properties [54].

The effect of temperature on crosslinked CMC/NPs colloidal solution results in the increase of viscosity as the temperature increases (Fig. 5(b)). At lower temperatures, the viscosity of crosslinked CMC/NPs appears to be constant. However, at temperature higher than 60 °C the crosslinking between CMC/NPs and CA begins to occur as CA requires heat treatment for the crosslinking process to take place [28]. Apart from that, the CMC pseudoplastic behaviour of CMC can also attribute to the increase of viscosity with the increase in temperature [7]. According to Liu et al. [31], the Piecewise Transition Mechanism suggest that macromolecule flow occurs due to the molecular chain's gravity centre displacement along the flow direction, allowing molecules to glide over each other between the molecular chains [31]. In the case of CA crosslinking reaction, with increased temperatures, it provides sufficient energy for CA molecules to glide, and increase molecular chain formation with CMC/NPs, thus increasing the viscosity of crosslinked CMC/NPs colloidal solutions by raising temperature.

For the dynamic rheological measurement, as indicated in Fig. 6(a) and (b), the storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) both increase with frequency. Based on the figures, it can be said that at low frequency, terminal G′ and G″ correspond with a homogenous polymer system [31] which indicates CMC and the nanoparticles forms a compatible colloidal solution system. The complex viscosity of crosslinked CMC/NPs can be observed to decrease as frequency increases (Fig. 6(c)), supporting the shear thinning behaviour. It can also be observed that the G′ value is higher than G″ (G′ > G″), suggesting that crosslinked CMC/NPs colloidal solutions are in a gel-like state connected by weak interactions such as hydrogen bonds and ester bonds and as the crosslinking increase, the viscoelastic of CMC/NPs increases [55]. This is also supported by a previous study where the possibility of an increase in G′ corresponds with the increase in crosslinking degree between CMC/NPs and CA [56].

From Fig. 7 , the non-Newtonian index of crosslinked CMC/NPs was determined and summarized in Table 1 . From the result obtained, it is apparent that crosslinked CMC/NPs exhibit shear thinning characteristics which describe as pseudoplastic as all samples have n < 1. In addition to that, all crosslinked CMC/NPs colloidal solution exhibit remarkable pseudoplastic behaviour as their n index have a higher departure degree from one [31]. Shear thinning or pseudoplastic behaviour is attributed to the disentanglement of crosslinked CMC/NPs chains, disaggregating large particle clumps, and its subsequent rearrangement in the flow direction [57]. At rest conditions, each particle and molecules are in the lowest energy consumption, allowing it to be in a three-dimensional coil shape and when force is introduced, the molecules are oriented to the shear direction causing the molecules to be disaggregated [58]. In the case of the crosslinked CMC/NPs colloidal solution, in the rest state, the crosslinked chains of CMC and nanoparticles, are entangled within each other. However, when the shear process is applied, the formed chains are oriented to the shear flow direction, resulting in chains disentanglement and reducing the viscosity (refer to Fig. 5(a) and Fig. 6(c)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial inhibition of CMC/NPs colloidal solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The test aims to observe and quantify the CMC/NPs colloidal solution's antibacterial properties. The bacteria growth density was measured using a UV–VIS spectrophotometer at 600 nm wavelength by using the spectrophotometer. The turbidity of bacteria cultures or OD were measured to quantify the amount of light absorbed by the bacterial suspension. In other words, as the bacterial cell concentration increases in the culture, the absorbance will also increase. The bacterial growth density of E. coli and S. aureus, along with the inhibitory percentage of colloidal solutions, are shown in Fig. 8 . It also showed that all colloidal solutions have shown significant inhibitory activity against both Gram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (S. aureus) bacterial strains. All colloidal solutions exhibited a 59–83 % inhibition percentage for E. coli and a 24–71 % inhibition percentage for S. aureus. The best results were obtained by CMC/AgNP colloidal solution, followed by CMC/GQD and CMC/GO. These results can be attributed to the size and mode of action of the nanoparticles.

From the figure, it can be observed that for all colloidal solutions, S. aureus exhibits lower inhibition activity compared to E. coli. In CMC with CA solution, the bacteria growth density measured are 2.3 and 3.2 for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. The solution has a weak inhibitory effect against E. coli and S. aureus, with 36 % and 14 % respectively. The inhibition can be attributed to the presence of CA, which weakened the outer membrane of E. coli [59], however, S. aureus capable to tolerate and adapting to stress caused by an acidic environment, therefore S. aureus exhibit a lower inhibition percentage in CMC with CA solution [28].

CMC/GO and CMC/GQD colloidal solutions showed an improved inhibition percentage compared to CMC with CA solution. The improvement shown by CMC/GO colloidal solution in the inhibition percentages of 59 % and 24 % for both E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. Meanwhile, CMC/GQD exhibits a slightly higher inhibition percentage and higher growth OD compared to CMC/GO colloidal solution with the inhibition percentage for E. coli is 64 % and S. aureus is 29 %. The antibacterial activity of CMC/GO can be explained due to GO acting as a nano-blade when in contact with the bacteria membrane surface that damaged the membrane by slicing it causing cytoplasmic leakage and cell death due to increasing amount of ROS [19]. However, a low inhibition percentage for S. aureus can be attributed to Gram-positive bacteria possessing a thicker peptidoglycan coating which prevents the bacterial cell from total contact with CMC/GO. The antibacterial action of GQDs in CMC/GQD colloidal solution is attributed to the Gaussian curvature match, which helps GQD attachment with the bacterial cell surface and rupture of the bacterial cell membrane. This leads to increased oxidative stress which caused membrane stress, that destroyed the cell barriers, resulting in cell content leakage and cell death [20]. Furthermore, the enhancement of GQD's antibacterial activity in CMC/GQD is possible due to CMC's ability to stabilise GQDs, allowing it to attach to the bacteria cell membranes [29].

A significant inhibition percentage was observed on both bacteria for CMC/AgNP colloidal solution with the inhibition percentage for both E. coli and S. aureus is 83 % and 71 %, respectively. CMC/AgNP colloidal solution show the highest inhibition activity is supported by the lowest bacterial growth OD reading of 0.6 for E. coli culture and 1.1 for S. aureus culture. Due to AgNPs' large surface area and ability to penetrate the cell nucleus, CMC/AgNP show a high inhibitory activity in both bacteria strains [26]. The inhibition of bacterial growth can be caused by the hindrance in bacterial DNA synthesis, protein inactivation, and inducing oxidative stress through ROS production [60,61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ATR-FTIR characterization of coated cotton thread with crosslinked CMC/NPs colloidal solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9(a) shows the overall ATF-FTIR spectra changes between CMC, CA and cotton thread coated with each CMC/NPs colloidal solution. From Fig. 9(b) the broad absorption band between 3650 and 3250 cm−1 represents the hydrogen functional group which confirmed the existence of –OH. This is consistent with the spectrum obtained, confirming the presence of the –OH group in the samples representing –OH stretching where the peaks are located at 3494 and 3278 cm−1 supported by previous findings [62].

After CA crosslinked with the CMC/NPs colloidal solution, a new peak appeared in 1743 and 1693 cm−1 , both corresponding to a carbonyl CO stretching [62,63]. These peaks are absent in the pure CT, CMC, and CA FTIR spectra. This band can be associated with the ester group [R1-C(O)O-R2] CO groups stretching which occurred during the crosslinking process indicating the crosslinking between CMC/NPs colloidal solution and CA has been successfully induced as stipulated in Fig. 10 . This also correlated with the results in effects of temperature in the static rheological properties of CMC/NPs, where at elevated temperature esterification process was induced between CA and CMC colloidal solutions.

From Fig. 10, the proposed mechanism postulate that the carboxylated groups of CA will dehydrate forming cyclic anhydride, which will further react with the –OH group of the CMC chains forming ester linkage [27,64]. The wavenumber at 1590 cm−1 in pure CMC indicates the presence of sodium carboxylate (–COONa) [64]. This peak disappears might be due to the displacement of sodium ion (Na+) and formation of CMC-NPs complex [23,42,43] during the crosslinking reaction with CA in the cotton thread coated with CMC/NPs colloidal solutions. This can be supported by the nanofluid stability monitored using UV–VIS photometric analysis in Section 3.1.

At 1423 cm−1 wavenumber the slight shift in the transmittance peak represent the COO− stretching vibration [4], this shifting indicates the molecules have reduced their mass due to the increase of frequency vibrations. Peak 1137 cm−1 ascribe to CO stretching alkyl substitution ether, and 1113 cm−1 to stretching of –COC [28,64]. The peak observed at 1053 cm−1 is the cellulose pyranose skeleton ring CO group stretching, whereas peak 897 cm−1 is due to the stretching of the 1,4-β-glycosidic bond stretching [19] and 770 cm−1 represents the C-OH of cellulose bond deformation [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of coated cotton threads
ce:section.ce:para: Based on Fig. 11 , it is found that cotton threads coated with crosslinked CMC/NPs colloidal solutions have higher tensile strength compared to pristine cotton thread and CMC-coated thread. The improvement in tensile strength for cotton thread coated with CMC/NPs solutions can be attributed to the homogeneity of nanoparticles dispersed in the CMC solution which supports the stability of nanoparticles in CMC. From the figure, the tensile strength and elastic modulus of CMC/GO coated thread increased from 0.112 MPa to 0.207 MPa and from 2.008 MPa to 4.705 MPa, respectively compared to pristine cotton thread. The increase in the tensile and elastic modulus is attributable to GO particles, which can carry an applied load owing to their stress transfer properties between the CMC matrix and GO which acts as a filler [37,65]. The tensile strength of the CMC/GQD coated thread increased by 10.1 % relative to the uncoated thread attributed to the oxygen-bearing functional group of GQD and the sp2 domain can generate an interfacial bond between GQD and CMC [66].

However, the MOE for both CMC/GO and CMC/GQD coated threads is lower than CMC and CMC/AgNP coated threads. The decrease in elastic modulus which might cause by the presence of an oxygenated group in both GO and GQD might cause repulsive interaction between oxygen-containing functional groups on GO and GQD surface and CMC [31]. Apart from that, it can also be observed that the elastic modulus of CMC/GQD is lower than CMC/GO. This might be due to the size effect between GO and GQD, where GQD have a much smaller size compared to GO. Therefore, the smaller size of GQDs may lead to a reduced reinforcement effect on the polymer matrix compared to larger and continuous GO sheets. The size-dependent mechanical properties of GQDs can result in a lower elastic modulus when incorporated into CMC.

CMC/AgNP coated thread showed the highest strength. A more compact structure has been formed due to the intra- and intermolecular interactions and crosslinking among cotton threads with CMCAgNP, resulting in increased tensile strength of CMCAgNP-coated thread [28]. Additionally, the uniformly dispersed AgNP in CMC/AgNP colloidal solution, allows AgNP plays a strengthening role in the cotton thread coating [67]. From the stability analysis, when AgNP is stabilized, the tendency of AgNP to agglomerate is reduced hence allowing the stress to be distributed evenly across the thread preventing concentrated stress on CMC/AgNP coated thread. Based on the results obtained for the mechanical strength of the cotton threads coated with CMC/NPs colloidal solutions, the tensile strength of the thread was enhanced by the presence of nanoparticles in the colloidal solutions, with CMC/AgNP coated thread exhibiting the highest tensile strength.

This study provided valuable insights between CMC colloidal solution's flow behaviour and the CMC-coated cotton thread viscoelasticity properties. However, there were some limitations associated such as that it did not fully represent the complex and dynamic environment encountered in wound dressing applications which wound exudate, temperature variations, and mechanical stress can affect the rheological properties of the coating material differently. Furthermore, the interaction with biological component such as wound exudate, variations in pH, and biological components would interact with the coating material and affect the coated cotton thread properties. However, based on these findings, it can be concluded that CMC can be a stabilizer for all nanoparticles, thus supporting the hypothesis of the study. The rheological properties of CMC and crosslinked CMC/NPs exhibited excellent pseudoplastic behaviour, suggesting that CMC solution can be tailored to suit specific applications, making it a versatile and widely used rheology modifier in various industries especially for biomedical coating solutions. The CMC/NPs coated threads showed enhanced mechanical properties compared to pristine thread and CMC-coated thread, with CMC/AgNP coated thread exhibiting the best tensile and antibacterial properties compared to CMC/GO and CMC/GQD. Therefore, further analyses such as FESEM, wettability properties, TGA, and antibacterial disk diffusion test will be continue to assess the application aspect of the cotton thread coated with CMC colloidal solution should be done to support the current findings for CMC/NPs as a potential candidate for an antibacterial coating of cotton thread into wound dressing applications.
